FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes ABCD Study (2016-2021) participants with non-missing screen use data at each year.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using growth mixture modeling to identify subgroups with similar screen use trajectories.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Preventive Medicine Reports (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use measured as composite: "sum of self-reported hours of daily use of television, videos, video games, social media, video chat, and texting." No per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM from TV/games/other devices. Fails criteria for multi-device/multi-activity composite bundling.

notes: Paper identifies two screen use trajectory classes and associates them with demographic characteristics and perceived discrimination, but the SM/phone variable does not meet FT-IC2 because it is a multi-device composite without per-modality isolation.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper examines ABCD baseline and follow-up data (waves 1-3) from 11,876 ABCD participants at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: K-means clustering to identify SMA patterns, linear mixed effects modeling to test associations with developmental measures and brain connectivity.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen Media Activity (SMA) measured with 14-item ABCD Youth Screen Time Questionnaire covering types of SMA (video entertainment, social communication, etc.). SMA paired with individual-trait-level variables: neurocognitive performance, behavioral problems, psychotic-like experiences, impulsivity, reward/punishment sensitivity, and brain functional connectivity. Qualifies as phones/SM modality with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: regression coefficients (β = -0.12 for neurocognitive, β = 0.13 for behavioral problems, β = 0.31 for psychotic experiences), 95% CI, FDR-corrected p-values (p < .001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses with numeric results for SMA patterns and behavioral/neural outcomes across multiple domains.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional at baseline and longitudinal follow-up with SMA as outcome measure; does not meet all three EC7 conditions (SMA appears as outcome but not exclusively in DV role across all analyses, and design includes cross-sectional components).
notes: Strong paper with SMA as primary exposure measured with specificity, paired with multiple individual-trait outcomes. Reports quantitative associations.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper develops and tests machine learning models using ABCD Study data, which recruited 10,000+ youth ages 9-10 at baseline across 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-validated machine learning models (PCR and LASSO) to predict ADHD symptoms using neurocognitive abilities, demographics, family characteristics, and screen time.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time included as predictor variable in models predicting parent- and teacher-reported ADHD symptoms (individual-trait outcome). Described as contributing meaningfully to prediction alongside neurocognition, sex, impulsivity, and parental monitoring.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported: explained variance in ADHD symptoms (15-20% for 1-year, 12-13% for 2-year predictions), R² values, feature contributions from PCR and LASSO models.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential models with numeric output: R² as model performance metric, feature importance weights indicating screen time's contribution to prediction.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (baseline predicting 1-year and 2-year outcomes); screen time is IV, not DV-only. EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs with SM as DV in every analysis and no SM-as-IV analysis.
notes: Machine learning prediction study with screen time as one feature among many. Screen time qualifies as individual exposure paired with ADHD symptoms (individual-trait outcome).
